Versification and Cantillation

This document is a companion to versification-differences.md. That document deliberately ignores cantillation, treating a verse as nothing more than a span of text between two cv-labels. That definition of a verse is what we will call a numbered verse in this document, where we will not ignore cantillation, and it will be important to make a distinction between a numbered verse and a chanted verse. We define a chanted verse as a span of text between two sof pasuq marks, where that span's accents obey the rules of cantillation.

In cases that are rare, but central to this document, a numbered verse is not a chanted verse. This happens mostly (and unavoidably) in the Decalogues, where, no matter what versification is used, some numbered verses are not chanted verses in at least one of the two strands of cantillation. Different trade-offs in the face of this "impossible" situation give rise to different versifications of the Decalogues.

(Aside: like many numbered verses of the Decalogues, Genesis 35:22 has two strands of cantillation and is only a chanted verse in one of those two strands. (In the other strand, it contains two chanted verses in their entirety.) Unlike in the Decalogues, at Genesis 35:22 all versifications discussed here (MAM, BHS, and Sefaria) make the same trade-off in the face of this "impossible" situation: they all agree to label Genesis 35:22 as a single numbered verse.)

Other than the cases in the Decalogues, the one other case in our versifications where a numbered verse is not a chanted verse is the Numbers 25/26 boundary in BHS versification. This case has little in common with the cases in the Decalogues. BHS splits the chanted verse that MAM calls Numbers 26:1 into a 25:19 span (a cv-label that does not exist in MAM) and a 26:1 span. BHS 26:1 starts right after an etnaḥta.

The Decalogues: background

Each of the Decalogues has two parallel strands of cantillation. The taḥton (תחתון) (lower) strand divides the passage into twelve chanted verses. The elyon (עליון) (upper) strand divides the passage into ten chanted verses, each traditionally taken to be one of the Ten Commandments. Relative to the taḥton, the lengths of the elyon verses are uneven. There are two elyon verses that are quite long, containing, respectively "three and a half" and four taḥton verses. (That "half" will be explained below.) On the other hand, there are four consecutive elyon verses that are very short: all four are contained within a single taḥton verse.

The Decalogues: two versification policies

A cv-label marks the start of a numbered verse. In the Decalogues, our versifications differ only in which chanted-verse starts have a cv-label:

  • MAM places a cv-label at the start of every taḥton verse.
  • BHS places a cv-label at the start of every taḥton or elyon verse — the union of the two cantillations' verse boundaries.
  • Sefaria is a hybrid of MAM and BHS: it places a cv-label at the start of every taḥton verse, and at the start of some but not all elyon verses.

From here on we discuss only MAM and BHS: describing Sefaria in detail adds nothing of interest, since it is merely a hybrid of the two. The Sefaria details can be found in the parent document, which treats versification without reference to cantillation.

Because BHS honors a superset of MAM's boundaries, every MAM Decalogue verse maps to one or more whole BHS verses, never the reverse.

The whole story is then how the two cantillations' boundaries sit relative to each other — and this differs between the two splits. At the early split they overlap (the splits interleave); at the late split the elyon is strictly contained within a single taḥton verse.

The early split — overlapping boundaries

When an elyon verse contains multiple taḥton verses in their entirety, there is no numbering drift between MAM and BHS. This is the case with the four consecutive taḥton verses in the table below, although unfortunately the point is somewhat muddied by a drift that has already happened, earlier in the Decalogues:

T זכ֛ור…לקדשֽו׃ ש֤שת…כל־מלאכתֽך׃ ויום֙…בשערֽיך׃ כ֣י…ויקדשֽהו׃
E זכור֩…לקדש֗ו ש֣שת…כל־מלאכת֒ך֒ וי֨ום…בשער֔יך כ֣י…ויקדשֽהו׃
M 20:7 20:8 20:9 20:10
B 20:8 20:9 20:10 20:11
t
e

(In the table above, each chanted verse's first word is shown in green and its last word in red. The bottom two rows give each taḥton verse's MAM and BHS number. All examples in this document are from the Exodus Decalogue; the appendix revisits these points in the Deuteronomy Decalogue, which shares the same structure but differs in its text.)

When an elyon verse contains only part of a taḥton verse, the numbering drifts between MAM and BHS. The table below shows the source of the drift encountered above: two elyon verses contain four taḥton verses in their entirety, but they are not contained "nicely".

T אנכי֙…עבד֑ים לא־יהי֥ה…על־פנֽי׃ לא־תעש֨ה…לאֽרץ׃ כ֣י…לשנאֽי׃ וע֥שה…מצותֽי׃
E אנכ֖י…עבדֽים׃ ל֣א…על־פנ֗י ל֣א…לא֗רץ לא־תשתחו֣ה…לשנא֑י וע֤שה…מצותֽי׃
M 20:2 20:3 20:4 20:5
B 20:2 20:3 20:4 20:5 20:6
t
e

The late split — nested boundaries

Here the two cantillations share the outer boundary and the elyon merely subdivides the interior. In MAM Exodus 20:12 the taḥton cantillation runs the four short commandments as a single verse; the elyon gives each its own verse — all four strictly contained within the one taḥton verse, both ending together at שקר:

T ל֥א…תרצ֖ח ל֣א…תנא֑ף ל֣א…תגנ֔ב לא־תענ֥ה…שֽקר׃
E ל֖א…תרצֽח׃ ל֖א…תנאֽף׃ ל֖א…תגנֽב׃ לא־תענ֥ה…שֽקר׃
M 20:12
B 20:13 20:14 20:15 20:16
t
e

(This table mirrors the early-split one, with the roles reversed. The four columns are the four short commandments in scripture order. The taḥton (T) row is now the single verse spanning all four — green on its first word, red on שקר, its interior plain — while each elyon (E) commandment is its own verse. The number rows show that MAM numbers once for the one taḥton verse (the spanning cells), whereas BHS numbers each elyon start.)

So BHS gives four verses where MAM has one. Deuteronomy 5:16 works identically.

Numbers 25/26: a break at an etnaḥta, not a sof pasuq

This boundary is cantillational in the opposite way. Here there is only one cantillation, and its verdict is negative: at the point BHS splits, cantillation marks not a verse end but a mid-verse etnaḥta.

MAM keeps Numbers 26:1 as a single chanted verse whose interior has a mid-verse paragraph break — a פסקא באמצע פסוק (here a petuḥah). The break is shown below as a line break, which is what a petuḥah ("open" section) in fact is: the text after it resumes on a fresh line. Both lines are one and the same chanted verse:

וַיְהִ֖י אַחֲרֵ֣י הַמַּגֵּפָ֑ה
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר … לֵאמֹֽר׃

The verse ends, as always, at sof pasuq (לֵאמֹֽר׃); the word before the break, הַמַּגֵּפָ֑ה, has only etnaḥta — the strongest mid-verse disjunctive, but not a verse end. BHS promotes that break to a verse and chapter boundary, making its 25:19 end at הַמַּגֵּפָ֑ה — on an etnaḥta, not a sof pasuq. So BHS's 25:19 is not a chanted verse; the MAM-simple data marks it ends-with-sampe: "pe2" and contents-corresponds-to: "less than a full verse in MAM".

Why did BHS make this a chapter boundary as well? The etnaḥta and its accompanying פסקא באמצע פסוק already signal a strong break here; perhaps someone nonetheless felt that a chapter break was needed to communicate an even stronger break at this point. Or perhaps this chapter break has its origin in a non-cantillated (perhaps even non-Hebrew) version of the text, where its defiance of the chanted verse boundary becomes irrelevant (or at least invisible).

Note what is and isn't doing the work. The פסקא באמצע פסוק is not the cause and is not part of the cantillation — such mid-verse paragraph breaks occur in many verses none of our versifications split (e.g. Deuteronomy 2:8); here it merely reinforces a division the etnaḥta already marks. And it is that etnaḥta that explains both sides: why there is a strong division to split on, and why a cantillation-sensitive versification like MAM's refuses to treat it as a verse end — a mid-verse pause, however strong, is still mid-verse.

The contrast with the Decalogues is the point: there, BHS's extra boundaries are real chanted-verse ends (in the other cantillation); here, BHS's extra boundary is not a chanted-verse end in any cantillation — it lands on an etnaḥta.

Summary

Passage MAM numbers at BHS numbers at Boundary relationship
Decalogues — early (Exod 20:2, Deut 5:6) taḥton starts taḥton or upper starts dual cantillation — overlapping
Decalogues — late (Exod 20:12, Deut 5:16) taḥton starts taḥton or upper starts dual cantillation — nested
Numbers 25/26 chanted-verse starts + mid-verse break (onto etnaḥta) break at etnaḥta, not sof pasuq

In the Decalogues, MAM's numbered boundaries coincide with taḥton chanted-verse ends, and BHS's extra boundaries are upper chanted-verse ends. In Numbers 25/26 BHS's extra boundary is not a chanted-verse end in any cantillation — it lands on an etnaḥta. That is the whole cantillational story behind these versification differences.

Appendix: the same points in Deuteronomy

Every example above is drawn from the Exodus Decalogue. The Deuteronomy Decalogue (Deuteronomy 5:6–5:17) carries the same two strands of cantillation with the same verse structure — the code that builds this document asserts as much against the Deuteronomy source — so the same points hold there. What differs are aspects of the wording (substituted words, added phrases) that leave those cantillation and versification points intact: the boundaries still fall in the same structural places, only on different words. This appendix re-shows the two points where that wording difference is visible, in Deuteronomy's own words. In the tables below a word is washed out when it is identical to its Exodus counterpart and shown at full strength when it differs, so the eye lands on the differences; first/last words keep their green/red hue, paled when they match Exodus.

The early split is not re-tabulated: through Deuteronomy 5:6–5:9 the text is word-for-word the Exodus text (bar vowel-pointing), so its table would be uniformly washed out. The differences live almost entirely in the Sabbath commandment, with only a connective וְ and one substituted word in the late split.

The Sabbath — a different, longer text

Deuteronomy's Sabbath commandment opens with שמור (not Exodus's זכור) and is considerably longer, so although the taḥton/elyon verse structure is unchanged — one elyon verse spanning four taḥton verses, exactly as in Exodus — most of the boundary words differ:

T שמ֛ור…אלהֽיך׃ ש֤שת…כל־מלאכתֽך׃ ויום֙…כמֽוך׃ וזכרת֗…השבֽת׃
E שמ֣ור…אלה֗יך ש֣שת…כל־מלאכת֒ך֒ וי֨ום…כמ֑וך וזכרת֞…השבֽת׃
M 5:11 5:12 5:13 5:14
B 5:12 5:13 5:14 5:15
t
e

Only the second taḥton verse (5:12) matches Exodus at both ends. The first (5:11) and fourth (5:14) differ at both ends; the third (5:13) shares its first word (ויום) but not its last word — Deuteronomy runs on past Exodus's בשעריך to כמוך.

The late split — nearly identical

Here the two Decalogues read very nearly alike. Deuteronomy joins the commandments with a connective וְ (וְלֹא, "and you shall not") where Exodus sets them without it, and its ninth commandment ends עֵד שָׁוְא where Exodus has עֵד שָׁקֶר. Those wording differences aside, the taḥton/elyon structure — four elyon verses nested in one taḥton verse, all ending together — is unchanged, so only וְלֹא and שָׁוְא stand at full strength while everything matching Exodus washes out:

T ל֥א…תרצ֖ח ול֣א…תנא֑ף ול֣א…תגנ֔ב ולא־תענ֥ה…שֽוא׃
E ל֖א…תרצֽח׃ ול֖א…תנאֽף׃ ול֖א…תגנֽב׃ ולא־תענ֥ה…שֽוא׃
M 5:16
B 5:17 5:18 5:19 5:20
t
e
